excerpt_en: After class, I went around art galleries in Dublin. As FOMUS, we held an art exhibition in Saitama Prefecture this May, and I think it would be really cool if we could hold one in Europe too, and it would help us expand even further. So
body_en:
After class,

I went around art galleries in Dublin.

As FOMUS,

we held an art exhibition in Saitama Prefecture this May,

and I think it would be really cool if we could hold one in Europe too,

and it would help us expand even further.

So, I don't have any connections or anything,

but I decided to just go around and check things out.

The artworks are already completed,

so what I need to do is:

  • Get them printed
  • Find picture frames
  • Find an art gallery
  • Find sponsors

That's the plan.

As for printing,

I found a place on Google and went there,

but they were unexpectedly closed today.

I'll try stopping by next time.

As for the frames,

I found one place that does custom measurements

tucked away in a hidden spot.

I discussed frame sizes and such there.

I also visited several art galleries.

However,

they weren't quite the type of gallery I was looking for,

so I need to keep searching.

Same goes for sponsors, of course.

If possible,

I'd love to get investors involved,

and while I don't know yet

how to approach them,

I'll figure something out.

I spent about 2 hours walking around,

but then it started raining heavily,

so I ducked into a general store.

While browsing around in there,

guess what!

They were selling picture frames!

Of course, since it's a general store,

they're not the same as professional frames,

but for making samples, these are more than enough.

When it's time to make high-quality pieces,

I'll need professional frames,

but until I find an art exhibition venue,

I figured simple frames would do just fine.

Taking a Chilean friend to a Japanese hair salon

From 4 PM,

I took a Chilean friend from language school to a hair salon.

This salon is the Japanese one run by Maru-san

that I went to before.

Plus, this time we went to the Temple Bar location.

We arrived a bit too early,

so we had a drink at a nearby pub!

We chatted and drank for about 30 minutes,

then headed to the salon.

By total coincidence,

the person cutting the hair turned out to be Korean,

so you had a Chilean getting a haircut at a Japanese salon

from a Korean stylist -- what a funny situation lol

Still,

he seemed to like the result, so that's great.

Card game event

After that, we moved to a hotel and

held a card game event at the same venue as the first time!

The game we used was, of course,

the card game "SILVA."

This time, 4 people showed up.

One was a Japanese person from the same language school.

And the other two were from Brazil and Greece.

The person from Brazil was the same one who came to the walking event last week.

Since everyone learned the rules from scratch,

I explained everything in English again this time.

Since I'd done this last week too,

teaching was easier than before.

Consistency really does matter!

After playing about 4 rounds,

everyone more or less got the hang of it.

Also, seeing the card game "SILVA"

being played with people from around the world

reminded me once again that I'm glad I created it.

A card game I made a year ago

is now being put to use like this,

and it's pretty moving to see it become a communication tool that connects people.

By chance, the person from Greece

works at a consulting company,

and they asked me all sorts of questions about SILVA.

Those questions were ones I'd already been asked many times in Japan, so it was nice to be able to answer them right away.

If my English improves a bit more,

things would go even more smoothly.

That said, I'm confident that through SILVA,

it's possible to spread the FOMUS brand.

https://www.notion.so

Investing in connections

After the event,

I headed to a Japanese meetup event.

It's the one I try to go to every week.

I've been here for 5 weeks now,

so I'm starting to see familiar faces.

Of course, there were lots of new people too.

And the Japanese person I happened to talk to

turned out to be someone participating in

the Medoc Marathon happening this week!! lol

I never expected to meet someone like that in Dublin!

He's an expat working here,

and he said he'd be running with his bosses from work.

It would be fun if we could meet on the day lol

And just like that,

it was another fulfilling day!

excerpt_en: 9/5 For about a month in Ireland, I've been doing daily grassroots outreach and running international exchange events, steadily building the foundation to expand the FOMUS brand in Europe. At this timing,
body_en:
9/5

For about a month in Ireland,

I've been doing daily grassroots outreach

and running international exchange events,

steadily building the foundation

to expand the FOMUS brand

in Europe.

At this timing,

starting today, I'll be spending a few days

in Bordeaux, France.

Since it's an evening flight,

I finished my classes

and headed straight to Dublin Airport.

What am I going for?

I've mentioned this a few times already,

but I decided back in February this year to participate in

the Medoc Marathon!

To briefly explain the Medoc Marathon:

"It's a crazy marathon where you run in costume while drinking wine -- a festival that fuses sports and a banquet."

That's basically it.

And yes, it's a full marathon (42 km).

Honestly, I never imagined

I'd "attempt a full marathon in my life,"

but after launching the FOMUS brand,

traveling all over Japan,

meeting so many people,

and throwing myself into all kinds of environments,

my own perspective expanded greatly,

and before I knew it,

I was signed up for the Medoc Marathon.

I can't believe life can become this exciting..

On top of that, this time,

some members who thought my Medoc Marathon challenge sounded fun are joining from Japan!

Sho-san, who I've been close with since meeting at an NFT event,

and Yusaku, a college student who enrolled at the same time as me during my Philippines study abroad earlier this year.

These two will be joining me.

Even though it's an absolutely wild event,

they found it just as interesting as I did

and went out of their way to spend time and money

to come all the way to France -- I'm nothing but grateful.

Of course, we're all marathon beginners!

#NoRecordsToSpeak

Since we all live in different places,

the three of us have never trained together even once,

but we're going to enjoy it to the fullest.

And personally,

I'll also be promoting the FOMUS brand

in the most natural, no-pressure way possible!

So with that,

having learned the lesson in Los Angeles

that you absolutely must not miss your flight,

I've been waiting at the airport since 6 hours before departure.

I also have a Priority Pass this time,

so I can use the lounges freely!

Dublin Airport has

two lounges,

and I tried both of them --

Terminal 1's is more spacious and comfortable!

The fact that waiting time at the airport becomes enjoyable

is one of the great things about Priority Pass!

All-you-can-eat food!

And you can drink too!

Experiences like these

might come in handy somehow

for future FOMUS activities,

so there's a lot to learn!

And with that, I'm off to France!

PS

The picture is a Japanese-style Medoc Marathon!

#PersonallyLoveThis

#WantToHostOneNextYear

excerpt_en: Arrived at Bordeaux Airport at 11:30 PM on the 5th. From there, I transferred by bus for about an hour and headed to today's hotel. At this point, I met up with FOMUS member Sho-san. From there, we talked for about 3 hours over beer and snacks
body_en:
Arrived at Bordeaux Airport at 11:30 PM on the 5th.

From there, I transferred by bus for about an hour

and headed to today's hotel.

At this point,

I met up with FOMUS member Sho-san.

From there, we talked for about 3 hours

over beer and snacks.

We had no particular plans in the morning,

so we took it easy.

Then at noon,

we met up with the other member, Yusaku.

Both of them came all the way from Japan

for the Medoc Marathon.

We were looking for lunch

and went into a French restaurant,

but the place was crowded,

so they weren't taking our order.

Meanwhile, customers who came in after us (Europeans) were getting their orders taken first.

So we asked a different staff member

to take our order,

but since they weren't our assigned server, they wouldn't do it.

We actually experienced discrimination against Asians.

At this rate, even if we placed our order,

I felt it would take over an hour for the food to come,

so we left. *before ordering

In situations like this,

you have to cut your losses,

otherwise you're just wasting your own time,

so we quickly moved on.

*I'm used to this kind of thing by now

*Of course I was still annoyed

So with that,

we went to a kebab shop where we could order easily

and get food quickly.

The kebabs came out in about 5 minutes lol

Way faster,

and they were super delicious.

After that, we did some light sightseeing around the city.

We visited the city's landmark and the cathedral.

Then we went back to the hotel once

and got ready.

After all,

the real deal starts here.

Medoc Marathon Eve Festival!

The Medoc Marathon takes place tomorrow on the 7th, but every year there are festivals before and after the event.

Since this is our first-ever Medoc, we decided to participate in every single event, so we headed to the eve festival.

The venue was about an hour and a half by bus from Bordeaux.

There was already a big crowd.

To enter the venue,

you needed a Medoc Marathon exclusive bracelet, but we hadn't picked up our bibs yet

and didn't have them on,

so we told the lady at the reception

and got spare bracelets.

You apparently can't get in without one of these

So with that,

let's go!!

Three types of white wine were served.

With the staff member who poured the white wine

There were surprisingly a lot of people!

Group photo of the three of us

There were even live musicians.

This was also a networking time, a chance to talk with people of various nationalities, so I went around striking up conversations.

Among them,

I met a Japanese woman.

When we talked,

she was attending the eve festival by herself,

and since we came as a group of three,

she wanted to join us,

so she ended up sitting with us at the participants-only dinner party!

Is this really the event before running 42 km?

The eve festival got into full swing!

The party venue and dinner were in the same place.

Music was blasting.

Then, once dinner started,

food and wine were served.

Since the theme was a pasta party,

various kinds of pasta were served.

We also chatted with the French lady sitting next to us!

The wine kept flowing!

These 4 bottles were served this time.

Honestly, I drank a ton lol

And before and after the meal,

there was a dance party,

the alcohol was kicking in,

and our spirits kept going up and up.. lol

Running 42 km tomorrow?

We had completely forgotten about that at this point lol

The Medoc Marathon is a festival.

It's an exciting party where whoever

fully enjoys the present moment wins.

I had the time of my life too.

However, there was one thing that was bugging us during the event.

That was the fact that

we still hadn't gotten our

Medoc Marathon race bibs.

Originally, we were supposed to pick up our bibs

at the venue by 11 PM on Friday (today),

but I had assumed that venue

and the eve festival venue were the same,

and was planning to pick them up then --

but apparently they were different.

So we left the eve festival at 10 PM

and took an Uber to the bib pickup venue.

However,

our bibs weren't there.

The likely explanation was that

since we booked the hotel through the official site,

they were delivered to our hotel.

We hadn't ordered them to be shipped,

but the materials did say to check at the hotel.

So, unable to pick them up,

we took an Uber back to Bordeaux.

By the way, at this point

we were riding back with Yoko-san, who we met at the eve festival.

The stars visible from the taxi on the way back

were absolutely beautiful.

When we got back,

we checked at the hotel front desk,

and sure enough, the bibs had been delivered!

What a relief!

Time to quickly shower and get to bed.

Well then, the Medoc Marathon

is finally tomorrow!

What's going to happen?

I can't wait!

excerpt_en: Medoc Marathon race day! Had breakfast at the hotel from 5:30 AM, got on the bus at 6:30, and headed to the venue in Pauillac. After about an hour and a half ride, we arrived, got ready, and headed to the starting line.
body_en:
Medoc Marathon race day!

Had breakfast at the hotel from 5:30 AM,

got on the bus at 6:30,

and headed to the venue in Pauillac.

After about an hour and a half ride,

we arrived,

got ready,

and headed to the starting line.

There were an absolutely insane number of people.

With the FOMUS members.

All three of us in matching uniforms

looked absolutely awesome!

And then, the start.

It had finally begun.

At first, the FOMUS members ran together as a group.

The first wine station was at around the 5 km mark.

This is how it was set up,

and we could just help ourselves.

What we'd decided beforehand was that

even though we were FOMUS members,

we knew our running paces

would be different,

so after the first wine station,

we'd each run at our own pace.

Running at someone else's pace

wouldn't benefit anyone.

So this time, we went at our own speeds.

I knew I was the slowest

of the three of us,

so the other two quickly disappeared from sight.

So from here on,

I was running solo.

That said,

with so many other participants around,

I was far from alone.

Plus, there were regular wine refills!

These came up

every few kilometers.

There were also regular water stations.

5 km, 10 km,

just kept running and running.

At one of the wine stations,

a handsome guy happened to take a photo for me.

*He wasn't a runner

Maybe he talked to me because I was alone?

Thanks to him,

I got a really great photo.

And so, I kept pushing forward.

Honestly, at the 20 km mark,

my legs were completely stiff,

and I was thinking, "Am I going to be okay?"

But still, I wouldn't finish if I didn't keep going,

so I forced my aching legs to move forward.

While I was eating snacks and drinking wine as usual, I spotted rows of small beds.

They actually had

massages!!

The Medoc Marathon is incredible!

I had no idea about this beforehand,

so I was genuinely surprised.

What an amazing service they include!

So naturally,

I decided to give it a try.

It was pretty nice.

Just being able to lie down

was a great break in itself.

It lasted about 5 minutes.

Once that was done, it was back to the race.

Since I got loosened up a bit,

I kept pushing on.

Wine just kept coming too.

After running so much,

I naturally got hungry,

so the snack stations were a lifesaver!

Personally,

the stations with fresh-cut oranges

were an absolute blessing!

And so, 30 km.

Run, drink, eat, repeat.

Honestly, I couldn't even tell the wine flavors apart lol

Kept going further.

And then,

what do you know,

there was another massage booth!!

Being a massage lover,

I of course went for it.

This time they worked on me for about 10 minutes.

With the massage lady.

*My hair is wet because

someone sprayed me with a hose about 2 minutes before this

As I kept going further,

the food also leveled up,

with potatoes and sausages appearing too.

Beer even showed up once.

This time, the weather was really unpredictable --

clear skies would suddenly turn cloudy,

or it would start pouring out of nowhere.

There were lots of muddy stretches on the road too.

And then, the 39 km mark.

Finally, oysters!!

I'd been wondering when they'd show up,

and they were right before the finish!! lol

I ate a ton!

Oysters, lemons, and white wine were all laid out together, and you could help yourself freely.

https://www.notion.so

Snapped a photo with a woman who happened to be at the same spot.

And as I kept going...

The finale was steak!!

This was SO good.

Red wine and meat are the ultimate combo.

And then, the final sprint!!

Honestly, my body was completely wrecked

and I was walking.

Others around me were walking too.

Then, about a kilometer before the finish,

rose wine.

Rose wine appeared right at the very end.

And at the same spot, there were grapes too.

These grapes were incredibly delicious.

And with that,

I somehow made it to the finish!!

I received the wooden medal and wine

that only Medoc Marathon finishers get.

And after 37 km apart,

I reunited with the FOMUS members.

Naturally, both of them had finished way before me.

https://www.notion.so

Participating in France's Medoc Marathon

with the FOMUS members

for my very first marathon,

and having all of us finish safely

was truly wonderful.

I was able to prove that the training

since February, when I decided to enter

the Medoc Marathon,

was not in vain.

After the finish,

there was all-you-can-drink beer,

so we all had a toast.

After that, I personally went around to other teams' areas and chatted with them.

Being able to do this kind of networking

is precisely because I've been in an English-speaking environment abroad,

which lets me confidently approach people.

And I can actually hold conversations!

That was so much fun.

Also, along the course,

so many people called out to me.

Japanese runners,

Japanese people who came to cheer,

of course,

but also foreigners who love Japan

and people who love sake.

That's because I ran with FOMUS masu attached to my outfit,

and I'm once again glad we went with these masu-adorned uniforms.

This marathon was truly packed with lessons.

Since everything was a first,

I wasn't sure how it would go,

but it all worked out.

And now,

I'll connect this experience to FOMUS's next activities.

If things go well,

next year or the year after,

I'd love to do a Sagi Island Medoc Marathon.

Using what I learned from the Medoc Marathon,

I could collaborate with all the people

I've met along the way.

There seems to be a lot of potential.

And just like that,

it was an incredible Medoc Marathon.

I'll make sure to put

this experience to good use

and keep spreading the FOMUS brand

to the world!!

excerpt_en: The Medoc Marathon doesn't end on race day alone -- there's an eve festival and even an after-party. Of course, not everyone participates, but since this was our first Medoc, we signed up for every event.
body_en:
The Medoc Marathon doesn't end on race day alone --

there's an eve festival and even an after-party.

Of course,

not everyone participates,

but since this was our first Medoc,

we signed up for every event.

The after-party was

a 10 km walking event & lunch

We departed early again today.

After about an hour and a half ride

to the same monastery as the eve festival, we arrived.

And without any formal start announcement or anything,

the walking just began on its own.

*Suddenly started

At the 10 km points,

wine was served.

You'd get a dedicated cup

and they'd pour wine into it for you.

*Exactly the same concept as FOMUS's neck-strap masu

I never expected the Medoc Marathon

to also use neck straps! lol

As FOMUS,

I'm getting tons of great hints.

Alright, let's keep going.

There were vineyards

as well as muddy patches.

The funny thing was,

including me, everyone's bodies were so wrecked

that their walking was a bit off. lol

Well, after running 42 km the day before,

that's only natural.

Tons of wine grapes everywhere too.

Many people came as part of group tours,

so the wait for wine was surprisingly long at times.

Since many had completed the Medoc Marathon,

lots of people were wearing

the bag that only finishers receive.

I also showed up with my Medoc finisher backpack!

It's surprisingly handy!

And while going around the wine stops,

something incredible happened!

A winery person

actually had a masu!!

It wasn't a FOMUS masu, of course,

but I was amazed to see someone with a masu!!

The reason this happened was because

I was wearing a masu necklace.

They noticed it

and ran to go get their masu!

As expected, masu make a huge impact overseas too!!

I made a connection with someone from a winery!

While walking today,

it rained heavily at times,

but I'd brought an umbrella so I was safe.

And the 10 km walk was complete!!

Cheers with white wine!

And then,

on to the lunch venue!

Again today, a massive crowd had gathered.

And by chance, a Singaporean couple ended up

sitting right across from us, so we had a toast!

We also chatted with other Japanese people sitting nearby,

and reunited with the French people

we'd befriended at the eve festival!

Making friends at events like these

is an absolute blast!

From there,

I went around chatting at various tables

and enjoyed the networking.

By the second half, I was pretty tipsy and in great spirits. lol

It was an incredibly fun after-party!

One takeaway though:

I thought the walking event would have

more opportunities for international exchange built in, but there wasn't really a structure for that, and you had to create those opportunities yourself.

The lunch event was easier since you were seated,

and conversation flowed naturally from there!

Regardless of which is better,

it was a learning experience!

Also, there were wine barrels set up

where you could pour your own!

That was so much fun too!

They had us entertained from start to finish!

And with that,

the entire Medoc Marathon schedule

came to a successful close!

Summary

Having participated in

all of the Medoc Marathon events,

I can say once again -- it was the best.

First of all,

completing my first-ever full marathon

was of course amazing,

but also being able to represent the FOMUS brand

and participate alongside members from Japan.

On top of that, getting to have so many

international exchanges.

Making friends.

And,

surprisingly, seeing that the "masu" was well-received.

I experienced firsthand that

the "masu" has real potential overseas

depending on the approach.

Also, personally,

despite this being a reckless challenge,

about half a year of training

got me to the point where I could

somehow finish,

and that boosted my confidence.

If you put in the effort, some things do work out

Not everything comes true through effort alone,

but you can raise the odds,

and this was definitely worth doing.

On other social media platforms too,

I posted about completing the Medoc Marathon,

and so many people

congratulated me.

And among my circle,

there are many people who've run the Medoc Marathon,

so I was now able to join that club too.

Whether this will directly lead to something right away,

I'm not sure,

but it was definitely a learning experience for me,

and I believe it was something essential

for expanding the FOMUS brand.

These three days

were full of lessons.

You can't understand these things without actually experiencing them, so being able to participate at this timing was really great.

If there's one regret,

it's that I wasn't able to pitch FOMUS

to the Medoc Marathon organizers.

Doing that during the marathon itself

turned out to be pretty difficult.

I'll think of

a different approach for that.

And so,

I completed my first-ever full marathon!

And at that, France's Medoc Marathon!

I never imagined I'd run a full marathon

in my life,

but with the influence of the environment

and the people around me,

I was able to run to the finish.

Using this experience,

I'll keep expanding the FOMUS brand even further!!

excerpt_en: With all the Medoc Marathon events finished, I had no particular schedule planned for today. Rather than setting aside a day for Bordeaux sightseeing, I'd left it open in case something came up from connections made at the Medoc Marathon.
body_en:
With all the Medoc Marathon events finished,

I had no particular schedule

planned for today.

Rather than setting aside a day

for Bordeaux sightseeing,

I'd left it open in case the connections from

the Medoc Marathon

led to something the next day.

So that's why

I'd kept my schedule open.

Unfortunately though, nothing like that happened,

and I had a free day on my hands.

So I switched gears

and decided to visit wine-related tourist spots in Bordeaux.

The wine museum

"Cite du Vin" and

the "Wine and Trade Museum."

At the Cite du Vin,

they used all kinds of technology

to exhibit and explain how wine is made,

with plenty of creative touches to keep you interested.

The only analog booth,

"Enjoy the Aroma,"

had lots of visitors trying it out.

As for the digital parts, some illustrations came up that I thought, "That was definitely made with AI!" which surprised me lol

#LookedFamiliar

With a total of 18 booths,

it was quite a rich experience that you could enjoy

both visually and through scent.

Unfortunately though,

there was no Japanese audio guide,

so it ended up being English listening practice for me.

The visit ended with a complimentary glass of wine!

A super satisfying museum!

On to the Wine and Trade Museum.

Next up, the Wine and Trade Museum.

This one was a lesser-known museum.

A wine tasting workshop inside the wine cellar.

The second glass came paired with

chocolate-coated raspberries.

I got to do the wine tasting together with

European gentlemen and ladies.

#MyWineTastingEtiquetteIsOnPoint

#OldHabitsFromWorkingAtAFrenchRestaurant

After that, I toured the cellar museum.

That said,

it wasn't that big,

so I finished quickly.

So with that,

the tour was over and I was leaving the venue,

when the girl at reception came chasing after me

asking if we could exchange social media.

I had no reason to refuse, so we swapped.

I also took a photo.

After that, with nothing else planned,

I decided to

head to a famous canele shop in Bordeaux.

The caneles were incredibly delicious,

but two of them cost over 7 euros.

Tourist prices and all,

but still, I was surprised.

Finally,

I went back to yesterday's hotel where I'd left my luggage,

and did a bit of work at the hotel bar.

My flight back to Dublin was the next morning (the 10th),

and I wanted to be at the airport by around 4 AM,

so I skipped getting a hotel and took the last train to the airport.

It was far from comfortable,

but hey, at least I was sheltered from rain and wind.

I waited here from 1 AM to 4 AM

while writing articles.

Final thoughts

This Bordeaux trip

was all about wine.

It's hard to believe it was only a 4-day stay

given how much I drank!

The Medoc Marathon was

a big challenge for me,

but as originally planned, I was able to create opportunities to spread the word about FOMUS,

and new business ideas came to me too.

On top of that,

there was plenty of international exchange

and the FOMUS brand gained more recognition!

Now I'm heading back to Dublin

and will continue

expanding FOMUS across Europe!

excerpt_en: Arrived in Ireland at 7 AM. I was out of the airport 30 minutes later, so I headed straight to language school. With all my luggage, of course lol. To be honest, I was so sleep-deprived that nothing from class was sinking in lol. After class,
body_en:
Arrived in Ireland at 7 AM.

I was out of the airport 30 minutes later,

so I headed straight to language school.

With all my luggage, of course lol

To be honest,

I was so sleep-deprived

that nothing from class was sinking in lol

After class,

I rushed straight home.

Then I finished some video review work

and took a nap.

I'd been pushing myself to the limit every day,

and I couldn't afford to catch a cold,

so I made time for sleep.

Thanks to that, I bounced right back!

excerpt_en: 9/11 As usual, I make sure to arrive at the language school by the 9 AM class start. Then something surprising happened... Just after 10 AM, I got a message from my homestay host mother. "My daughter is back, so I need you to free up the bed today. I've called a taxi at 6 PM, so please use that to move."
body_en:
9/11

As usual, I make sure to arrive

at the language school

by the 9 AM class start.

Then something surprising happened...

Just after 10 AM, I got a message from my homestay host mother.

"My daughter is back, so I need you to free up the bed today. I've called a taxi at 6 PM, so please use that to move."

Huh?

I had no idea about any of this??

Plus, I hadn't gotten

any word from the agency either.

It made absolutely no sense.

While I was mulling this over during class,

an email from the agency arrived.

The subject was, of course, "the move."

Due to a family situation, I needed to urgently transfer to a different homestay, they said.

Normally, such a sudden

move would be unthinkable,

but since this was the second time, I was pretty used to it.

However, starting this evening,

I had an event I absolutely couldn't miss,

and there was no way I could be home by 6 PM.

Being told to move on the very same day,

while already at school,

with a taxi called for 8 hours later --

I couldn't let that throw off my plans.

So I negotiated.

When I told the host mother I absolutely couldn't make it, she said I'd need to change the taxi time through the agency.

So I immediately contacted the agency.

By the way, the Japanese agency was unresponsive due to the time difference -- completely outside business hours.

Honestly, they're useless.

The one I've been communicating with every time

is the parent organization above the Japanese agency.

After explaining the situation,

I was able to change the pickup

to 10:30 PM.

At least that was a relief.

Sake event

Now here's the main event for today.

Third visit to "DARUMA," a Japanese restaurant in Dublin.

My first time going alone.

Today, DARUMA was hosting

a sake event,

limited to 12 guests.

The earliest slot at 5:30 PM

was limited to 3 people, and 2 had cancelled,

so I got to have a nice, in-depth conversation with the bartender, Yu-san.

For me, that was actually ideal.

The food was served paired with sake.

It had been ages since I'd had sushi,

and they had charcoal-grilled yakitori too!

First time in about two and a half months!!

On top of that, getting to drink sake was a luxury.

I even ordered additional sake.

Price-wise it might seem expensive by normal standards, but for me, the value lies in connecting and building relationships with Japanese people active in Dublin.

So this time, I paid

to enjoy great food

while also strengthening those connections.

It's not about immediately offering masu or anything --

first, it's important to be accepted

simply as "MaSU" the person.

It was a truly fun and educational experience.

Off to the Wednesday Japanese meetup

Since I had a bit of time before the taxi,

I went to the meetup until the last possible minute.

I enjoyed it here until 9 PM,

then took a 40-minute taxi ride.

Got home at 10 PM.

Packed in 15 minutes from there!

Made it to the taxi just in time.

This time, I was heading north instead of south.

And so, I greeted my 3rd homestay family.

Since it was already late,

I just said hello and went to bed.

More details tomorrow!

title_en: First Masu Photo Shoot in a While!
excerpt_en: After finishing class, I had an online meeting for FOMUS-related work. After that was done, I had a Masu Photo shoot for the first time in a while. The subject was an Indian person who's been living in Ireland for about 3 years. This time too, we went around the city center
body_en:
After finishing class,

I had an online meeting for FOMUS-related work.

After that was done,

I had a Masu Photo shoot for the first time in a while.

The subject was an Indian person

who's been living in Ireland for about 3 years.

This time too,

we went around the city center for the shoot!

I really want to do Masu Photo shoots

much more frequently,

but since I handle all the shooting and editing myself,

it's hard to do unless I have a decent block of time.

That's the current challenge.

After mid-October,

I'll be graduating from language school,

so I'll have relatively more free time on weekdays.

At that point,

I'm hoping to do Masu Photo shoots

with students and models here in Ireland.
